meta.certificationName: Databricks Certified Generative AI Engineer Associate
meta.title: DBX-GAIA-3: Agent Development and Responsible AI

questions.stem: A data science team wants to build a code assistant that can answer questions about a company's internal proprietary codebase of 500,000 lines of code. The codebase is stored in a private Git repository and changes daily. Which approach is MOST suitable?

options.text: Fine-tune an LLM on the full codebase once per quarter
options.explanation: Quarterly fine-tuning would miss daily changes and is expensive to rerun frequently.

options.text: Build a RAG pipeline that indexes code files and docstrings in a vector store, refreshed nightly from the repository
options.explanation: RAG allows the system to retrieve only the relevant code snippets for each query from the indexed codebase, keeping knowledge current with nightly refreshes and avoiding the need to retrain.

options.text: Paste the entire codebase into the LLM's context window on every query
options.explanation: 500,000 lines of code far exceeds any current context window limit.

options.text: Use a general-purpose coding LLM without any additional codebase context
options.explanation: A general-purpose LLM has no knowledge of proprietary internal codebases.

questions.stem: An engineer is configuring embeddings for a RAG pipeline. The documents are long technical specifications (average 10,000 words each). The team decides to chunk documents before embedding. Which chunk size / overlap combination is MOST balanced for technical specifications?

options.text: chunk_size=32, chunk_overlap=0 — very small chunks for maximum precision
options.explanation: 32 tokens is too small to convey meaningful technical content — most sentences exceed that limit.

options.text: chunk_size=8192, chunk_overlap=0 — entire document as one chunk to preserve all context
options.explanation: Chunk_size=8192 creates chunks that are too broad — the entire specification becomes one chunk, making retrieval imprecise.

options.text: chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=512 — fully overlapping chunks to prevent any information loss
options.explanation: Chunk_overlap cannot exceed chunk_size; fully overlapping chunks would mean every chunk is identical to its neighbor, which is nonsensical.

options.text: chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64 — medium chunks with overlap to preserve context at boundaries
options.explanation: 512 tokens is a widely used chunk size that is large enough to contain a meaningful passage but small enough to be topically focused. An overlap of 64 tokens (about 12.5%) prevents important information at chunk boundaries from being split without creating excessive redundancy.

questions.stem: A team is choosing between the BGE-Large and BGE-Small embedding models for their Databricks Vector Search index. BGE-Large produces 1024-dimensional vectors; BGE-Small produces 384-dimensional vectors. Which statement BEST describes the trade-off?

options.text: BGE-Large produces higher-quality embeddings with better semantic representation, but requires more storage and has higher embedding computation cost; BGE-Small is faster and cheaper but may have lower retrieval quality on complex queries
options.explanation: Larger embedding models generally produce richer, more nuanced semantic representations (higher NDCG@10 on retrieval benchmarks), but they cost more to compute and store. BGE-Large at 1024 dimensions requires approximately 2.7x more storage per vector than BGE-Small at 384 dimensions, and embedding computation takes longer. The right choice depends on quality requirements versus cost and latency constraints.

options.text: BGE-Small is always preferable because lower-dimensional embeddings search faster and the quality difference is negligible
options.explanation: The quality difference is not negligible on complex technical queries — BGE-Large measurably outperforms BGE-Small on retrieval benchmarks.

options.text: BGE-Large can only be used with cosine similarity; BGE-Small supports both cosine and dot product
options.explanation: Both models support standard similarity metrics including cosine similarity and dot product.

options.text: BGE-Small supports longer input sequences than BGE-Large, making it better for long documents
options.explanation: Maximum input sequence length is a separate property from embedding dimensionality and is similar for both BGE variants.

questions.stem: A team is designing a production RAG system. Select TWO design decisions that directly affect the latency of the retrieval step (before the LLM call). (Select TWO)

options.text: The number of chunks retrieved (top-k value)
options.explanation: A higher top-k requires the vector search engine to score more candidates and return a larger result set, increasing retrieval latency.

options.text: The temperature parameter set on the LLM endpoint
options.explanation: The LLM temperature only affects the generation step, not retrieval.

options.text: Whether the vector index uses approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) or exact search
options.explanation: Exact nearest neighbor search scans all vectors and is O(n), while ANN algorithms (e.g., HNSW) use approximations to achieve sub-linear search time at the cost of perfect recall.

options.text: The number of few-shot examples in the system prompt
options.explanation: Few-shot examples are part of the LLM prompt and only affect the generation step's latency (more input tokens to process), not the vector retrieval step.

questions.stem: A team is choosing how to access foundation models in Databricks for a new application. Which statement BEST describes when **pay-per-token** is preferred over **provisioned throughput**?

options.text: Choose pay-per-token when the workload is still being explored or traffic is variable, because it avoids committing dedicated serving capacity up front
options.explanation: Databricks positions **pay-per-token** as a good fit for experimentation and workloads where you do not want to commit infrastructure up front. **Provisioned throughput** is the better fit when you need dedicated capacity and performance guarantees for production workloads.

options.text: Choose pay-per-token when strict production latency guarantees are required for a steady high-volume workload
options.explanation: Those requirements point toward provisioned throughput, not pay-per-token.

options.text: Choose pay-per-token only when using external models, because Databricks-managed foundation models require provisioned throughput
options.explanation: Databricks-managed foundation models are also available through pay-per-token endpoints.

options.text: Choose pay-per-token when you need to deploy a fine-tuned model with reserved throughput and performance guarantees
options.explanation: Deploying fine-tuned models with reserved capacity and performance guarantees is a provisioned throughput use case.

questions.stem: A team is designing a multi-agent GenAI system where a 'router' LLM decides which specialist agent (search agent, calculator agent, or document summarizer) should handle each user query. What is the PRIMARY benefit of this architecture over a single monolithic LLM?

options.text: Multi-agent systems always produce better answers because multiple models vote on the response
options.explanation: Ensemble voting is not inherent to multi-agent architecture — agents are routed, not polled.

options.text: Multi-agent systems eliminate hallucination because agents can check each other's work
options.explanation: Individual agents still hallucinate; peer review between agents reduces but does not eliminate errors.

options.text: Each specialist agent can be optimized independently for its specific task — using the right model size, tool access, and prompt template — resulting in better performance and cost efficiency than a single generalist LLM trying to do everything
options.explanation: Decomposing a complex task into specialized sub-agents allows each component to be optimized independently. A search agent might use a small, fast model with vector search; a calculator agent might use a code-execution tool with a deterministic engine; a summarizer might use a larger model with a long context window. This is more efficient than forcing a single model to handle all task types with a single prompt strategy.

options.text: Multi-agent systems require no RAG because each agent has its own fine-tuned knowledge
options.explanation: Individual agents may still need RAG for knowledge-intensive sub-tasks.

questions.stem: A team wants to use Databricks Foundation Model APIs to generate text embeddings for their RAG pipeline without deploying their own model. Which model is available as a managed embedding endpoint in Databricks Foundation Model APIs?

options.text: text-embedding-ada-002 (OpenAI)
options.explanation: Text-embedding-ada-002 is an OpenAI model accessible via their API or through the Databricks AI Gateway routing to OpenAI, not a native Databricks Foundation Model.

options.text: sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 (local HuggingFace)
options.explanation: Sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 is a HuggingFace model that requires custom deployment via Model Serving.

options.text: cohere-embed-english-v3.0
options.explanation: Cohere-embed-english-v3.0 is a Cohere model accessible via AI Gateway or directly through Cohere's API, not a native Databricks managed endpoint.

options.text: databricks-bge-large-en
options.explanation: Databricks-bge-large-en is one of the embedding models available as a managed Foundation Model API endpoint in Databricks — accessible via the mlflow.deployments client or the REST API without any model deployment configuration.

questions.stem: A RAG system retrieves the top 5 chunks for each query, but an engineer observes that the first 2 chunks are almost always highly relevant while chunks 3-5 are often noise. Adding all 5 chunks to the prompt increases token usage and distracts the LLM. What technique can address this WITHOUT reducing top-k to 2?

options.text: Use a cross-encoder reranker to score all 5 retrieved chunks by their relevance to the specific query, then pass only the top 2 reranked chunks to the LLM
options.explanation: Bi-encoder retrieval (used in vector search) trades precision for speed — it retrieves a candidate set quickly but the ranking may not be perfect. A cross-encoder reranker takes the specific query and each candidate chunk as a pair and computes a more accurate relevance score, often significantly improving ranking quality. The team can retrieve top-10 from the vector search, rerank with a cross-encoder, and pass only the top-2 reranked results to the LLM — getting the benefit of broad initial retrieval with precise final selection.

options.text: Increase the chunk size so fewer chunks are needed
options.explanation: Increasing chunk size addresses context completeness, not noise filtering — larger chunks do not improve the precision of ranking.

options.text: Switch the similarity metric from cosine to dot product to improve ranking order
options.explanation: Changing the similarity metric does not add a reranking step; it may change absolute scores but not reliably eliminate noise chunks.

options.text: Add a metadata filter to exclude chunks not tagged with the current query topic
options.explanation: This requires pre-defined topic metadata which may not exist and does not dynamically score relevance to the specific query.

questions.stem: A developer needs to implement a conversational RAG system where follow-up questions like 'What about the second point?' implicitly refer to the previous response. How should the retrieval query be formulated for follow-up questions?

options.text: Use only the follow-up question as the vector search query, ignoring conversation history
options.explanation: 'What about the second point?' as a standalone query will retrieve poor results — the vector space has no way to resolve what 'the second point' refers to.

options.text: Concatenate all previous turns into a single long query for the vector search
options.explanation: Concatenating all turns creates a long, noisy query that dilutes the specific intent of the follow-up.

options.text: Use a 'query reformulation' step where an LLM rewrites the follow-up question into a standalone question using conversation history, then use that as the retrieval query
options.explanation: Follow-up questions like 'What about the second point?' are not self-contained — they require the prior context to be meaningful as a search query. A query reformulation step (sometimes called 'contextualized query generation') uses a fast LLM call to rewrite the follow-up as a standalone question ('What is the second recommendation from the product roadmap described earlier?'), which then produces a much better embedding for vector search.

options.text: Disable retrieval for follow-up questions and rely on the LLM's parametric memory
options.explanation: The LLM's parametric memory does not contain information from the retrieved documents in the current session.

questions.stem: A team needs to extract text from Word documents (.docx format) for a RAG pipeline in Databricks. Which approach is correct?

options.text: Use spark.read.text() to read .docx files directly into a Spark DataFrame
options.explanation: Spark.read.text() reads files as raw UTF-8 text; a .docx file would appear as binary garbage since the format is not plain text.

options.text: Use COPY INTO to load .docx files into a Delta table and rely on Databricks to parse the format automatically
options.explanation: COPY INTO is designed for structured data formats such as CSV and JSON, not binary document formats.

options.text: Convert .docx files to images and use a vision-language model to extract text
options.explanation: Converting to images introduces OCR error risk and is unnecessarily complex when purpose-built text extraction libraries exist.

options.text: Use a Python library like python-docx or the LangChain Docx2txtLoader to extract text from each .docx file, then process the extracted text in a Databricks notebook or UDF
options.explanation: .docx files are binary ZIP archives containing XML markup — they cannot be read as plain text by Spark. A Python library such as python-docx or LangChain's Docx2txtLoader extracts the text content from the binary format. This can be applied at scale in Databricks using a Python UDF or a pandas UDF on a DataFrame of file paths.

questions.stem: A team is preparing metadata to enable filtered search in their RAG system. They want users to be able to filter retrieved documents by 'department' and 'document_date'. What is required to enable these filters in Databricks Vector Search?

options.text: Create separate vector indexes for each department to enable isolation
options.explanation: Creating separate indexes per department is operationally complex and does not support cross-department searches.

options.text: Include 'department' and 'document_date' as columns in the source Delta table, and specify them as filterable columns when creating the Vector Search index
options.explanation: Databricks Vector Search supports structured metadata filtering, but the filterable columns must be explicitly declared at index creation time. The metadata columns ('department', 'document_date') must exist in the source Delta table and be registered as filter columns when creating the index. At query time, the filters are passed as key-value pairs to similarity_search().

options.text: Append the department name and date to the text of each chunk before embedding so the embedding captures this information
options.explanation: This workaround (called 'stuffing metadata into text') degrades embedding quality without providing reliable filter semantics at query time.

options.text: Use a WHERE clause in the SQL query that creates the source Delta table to pre-filter documents
options.explanation: The Delta table itself is not being queried with SQL at search time — the vector index is queried instead.

questions.stem: A team embeds documents using Model A during initial indexing. Six months later, they upgrade to Model B, which produces embeddings in a different vector space with different dimensionality. What must they do to the existing index?

options.text: Add the new embeddings from Model B as an additional column in the same index; Databricks Vector Search will use both automatically
options.explanation: Vector Search does not support mixing embedding models in a single index for comparison — both dimensionality and semantic space differ.

options.text: Re-embed all existing documents using Model B and rebuild the vector index from scratch, since embeddings from different models are not comparable
options.explanation: Vector similarity search only produces meaningful results when the query embedding and the document embeddings are from the same model. Different embedding models produce vectors in different semantic spaces — a query embedded with Model B cannot be meaningfully compared against documents embedded with Model A. When upgrading embedding models, all documents must be re-embedded with the new model and the index rebuilt.

options.text: Apply a linear transformation to map Model A embeddings to Model B's vector space
options.explanation: A linear transformation between arbitrary neural network embedding spaces is not a reliable method and is generally not supported in production systems.

options.text: Only re-embed documents added after the upgrade; older documents are compatible with Model B automatically
options.explanation: There is no automatic compatibility between embeddings from different models — all vectors must be in the same embedding space.

questions.stem: A team is chunking research papers that use a standard academic structure (Abstract, Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion). Select TWO benefits of splitting by section heading rather than by fixed character count. (Select TWO)

options.text: Each chunk corresponds to a semantically coherent section, making retrieval more precise — a query about methodology retrieves the Methods section
options.explanation: Aligning chunks with document sections (Abstract, Methods, Results) creates units that correspond to conceptually distinct parts of the paper. This dramatically improves retrieval precision — a query asking about experimental setup retrieves the Methods section rather than a random character-count slice that spans sections.

options.text: Section-based chunks guarantee uniform token counts, simplifying batch embedding
options.explanation: Academic sections vary greatly in length — Methods may be 5,000 tokens while the Abstract is 250, so chunk sizes are emphatically not uniform.

options.text: Section metadata (e.g., section='Methods') can be stored alongside each chunk for metadata-filtered retrieval
options.explanation: Section-based splitting naturally generates section-name metadata that can be attached to each chunk, enabling powerful filtered retrieval (e.g., filter to only search 'Results' sections when a user asks about experimental outcomes).

options.text: Section-based splitting eliminates the need for any overlap between chunks
options.explanation: Overlap may still be needed at sub-section boundaries if very long sections are further split.

questions.stem: An engineer writes a Spark UDF to embed document chunks using the Databricks Foundation Model API. During testing with 1,000 documents, the UDF works correctly. During production with 10,000,000 documents, they encounter rate limit errors from the embedding API. What is the BEST mitigation?

options.text: Increase the cluster size to add more executor cores, which will send more parallel requests to the API
options.explanation: Adding more executor cores would actually worsen the problem by generating even more concurrent requests against the same rate limit.

options.text: Reduce the chunk size so each API call processes fewer tokens
options.explanation: Reducing chunk size changes the data structure, not the number of API calls — the number of requests stays the same.

options.text: Switch to a non-Databricks embedding API that has no rate limits
options.explanation: 'no rate limits' is not a realistic property of any production API; all production APIs impose rate limits.

options.text: Add retry logic with exponential backoff in the UDF, and consider batching multiple texts per API call and setting Spark parallelism to stay within the API's rate limits
options.explanation: The rate limit error is caused by too many concurrent API calls from the distributed Spark executors. The solution is to: (1) add exponential backoff and retry logic to handle transient rate limit errors gracefully, (2) batch multiple texts per API call to reduce the number of requests while maintaining throughput, and (3) tune Spark parallelism (spark.default.parallelism, repartition()) to control the concurrent request rate against the API limits.

questions.stem: A team stores document embeddings in a Delta table with the following schema: id STRING, text STRING, embedding ARRAY<FLOAT>, source_url STRING, created_date DATE. They create a Databricks Vector Search Delta Sync index on this table. Which column must be designated as the primary key when creating the index?

options.text: text, because the content is the most important column
options.explanation: 'text' is not guaranteed to be unique (duplicate chunks could have identical text) and using content as a key is not appropriate for a primary key.

options.text: embedding, because the index is built on the vector values
options.explanation: 'embedding' is an ARRAY<FLOAT>, not a STRING, and vector values are not unique identifiers.

options.text: id, because it is a STRING type and serves as the unique identifier for upserts and deletes
options.explanation: Databricks Vector Search requires a primary key column of type STRING to uniquely identify each row. The 'id' column satisfies both requirements — it is a STRING type and acts as the unique identifier. The vector search engine uses this ID column to manage upserts and deletes when the Delta Sync pipeline propagates changes from the source table.

options.text: source_url, because it uniquely identifies the document source
options.explanation: 'source_url' is a STRING but likely not unique at the chunk level — multiple chunks from the same document share the same source URL.

questions.stem: A team uses the Databricks VectorSearchClient to query their index. They call client.get_index('catalog.schema.my_index').similarity_search(query_text='What is the refund policy?', columns=['id', 'text', 'department'], num_results=5). What does the num_results parameter control?

options.text: The maximum number of tokens in each returned document chunk
options.explanation: Token limits per chunk are determined at indexing time by the chunking strategy, not at query time.

options.text: The number of nearest neighbor chunks returned by the vector search (top-k)
options.explanation: Num_results in the Databricks Vector Search API's similarity_search() method specifies the number of most similar chunks to return (equivalent to top-k in nearest neighbor search terminology). Increasing num_results returns more candidate chunks but also increases the amount of context passed to the LLM.

options.text: The number of parallel search threads used by the index
options.explanation: Parallelism is an internal index configuration, not a per-query parameter exposed to the caller.

options.text: The number of columns returned in the result set
options.explanation: The columns returned are controlled by the 'columns' parameter, not 'num_results'.

questions.stem: A developer is designing a prompt template for a customer-facing RAG chatbot. The system prompt should ensure the model stays focused on product support topics. Which system prompt pattern is MOST effective?

options.text: You are a helpful assistant. Answer any question the user asks.
options.explanation: 'any question' is too broad — it invites off-topic queries and hallucination on topics outside the retrieved context.

options.text: You are a product support assistant for Acme Corp. Answer questions using ONLY the provided context. If the question is not about Acme products or is not addressed in the context, respond: 'I can only help with Acme product questions. Please contact support@acme.com for other inquiries.'
options.explanation: It specifies: (1) the assistant's role and company context, (2) the instruction to use ONLY the provided context (grounding), and (3) a clear fallback behavior for out-of-scope questions with a specific response template. This prevents topic drift, reduces hallucination, and provides users with a useful next step when the bot cannot help.

options.text: You are an AI. Never make mistakes.
options.explanation: 'Never make mistakes' is a vague, unenforceable instruction that does nothing to constrain scope or improve grounding.

options.text: Answer the question. Do not use the context.
options.explanation: It actively instructs the model to ignore the retrieved context, undermining the entire purpose of RAG.

questions.stem: A team deploys their LangChain RAG chain as a model using mlflow.langchain.log_model() and registers it in the MLflow Model Registry. Select TWO benefits of this approach over deploying the chain as a custom FastAPI service. (Select TWO)

options.text: The model can be versioned and promoted through staging environments (Staging → Production) using the MLflow Model Registry lifecycle
options.explanation: The MLflow Model Registry provides a governance workflow for model versioning, annotations, and stage transitions (None → Staging → Production → Archived). Teams can compare versions, review changelogs, and gate production deployments through the registry.

options.text: MLflow automatically eliminates hallucinations in the registered model
options.explanation: MLflow is a tracking and serving platform, not a hallucination prevention system.

options.text: The registered chain can be deployed directly to Databricks Model Serving with a single command, without writing REST API server code
options.explanation: Models registered in MLflow can be deployed to Databricks Model Serving with mlflow models serve or directly through the UI, without writing any HTTP server code — MLflow handles the serving infrastructure.

options.text: Registering in MLflow Model Registry removes the need for any environment dependencies
options.explanation: Environment dependencies (conda environments, pip requirements) must still be specified and are captured in the model's MLmodel artifact.

questions.stem: A team builds a LangChain LCEL chain: retriever | format_docs | prompt | llm | StrOutputParser(). During a load test, they notice that the retriever step is the bottleneck. Which optimization would MOST effectively reduce the retrieval step latency?

options.text: Switch the Vector Search index from exact search to ANN (approximate nearest neighbor) search to trade a small amount of recall for significantly lower latency
options.explanation: The vector search retrieval step's latency is dominated by the search algorithm. Exact search is O(n) in the number of indexed vectors, while ANN algorithms like HNSW run in O(log n) time. Switching from exact to ANN search can reduce retrieval latency by orders of magnitude on large indexes, typically with only a 1-5% reduction in recall.

options.text: Increase the LLM's max_tokens parameter to generate shorter responses
options.explanation: Max_tokens controls the generator LLM output length, not the retriever.

options.text: Use a larger embedding model to improve embedding quality
options.explanation: A larger embedding model would increase the query embedding computation time, not improve retrieval speed.

options.text: Add more few-shot examples to the prompt template
options.explanation: Few-shot examples only affect the LLM prompt, not the retrieval step.

questions.stem: A developer is configuring the Databricks AI Gateway to route requests to multiple LLM providers. They want certain high-priority enterprise users to always route to GPT-4, while standard users route to a cheaper model. Which Databricks AI Gateway feature supports this?

options.text: Configure separate AI Gateway routes for each user tier, and use application logic to call the appropriate route based on user tier
options.explanation: The Databricks AI Gateway does not natively route based on user identity — it routes based on the endpoint/route called. The standard pattern for user-tier-based routing is to configure separate routes (e.g., '/gpt4-route' and '/standard-route') and have the application layer select the appropriate route based on the user's tier, which can be determined from authentication metadata.

options.text: Enable automatic model selection in the AI Gateway, which reads user role from the JWT token and routes accordingly
options.explanation: The AI Gateway does not natively read JWT claims to dynamically select models — that routing logic belongs in the application layer.

options.text: Use the AI Gateway's built-in user prioritization feature, which automatically upgrades high-priority users to premium models
options.explanation: No such 'user prioritization' feature exists in the Databricks AI Gateway.

options.text: Set a higher rate limit for premium users so they automatically receive GPT-4 responses
options.explanation: Rate limits control throughput volume, not model selection or routing.

questions.stem: A team uses DatabricksEmbeddings in LangChain to embed the user's query before calling similarity_search(). The developer notices that the query embedding step accounts for 200ms of latency per request. What is the MOST impactful optimization for this specific step?

options.text: Cache embeddings for common queries using an in-memory cache (e.g., LangChain's CacheBackedEmbeddings with a Redis backend)
options.explanation: Many real-world applications have a small set of frequently repeated queries (e.g., 'What are your business hours?', 'How do I reset my password?'). Caching embeddings for these common queries avoids re-computing the same embedding repeatedly, eliminating the 200ms latency hit for cache hits. LangChain provides CacheBackedEmbeddings that wraps any embedding model with a configurable cache backend.

options.text: Switch from BGE-Large to BGE-Small to reduce the embedding dimensionality and computation time
options.explanation: is a valid optimization that reduces computation cost, but caching has a higher impact for high-frequency repeated queries — it eliminates the embedding call entirely rather than just speeding it up.

options.text: Pre-embed all possible user queries and store them in the vector index
options.explanation: The set of possible user queries is infinite and cannot be pre-computed ahead of time.

options.text: Run the embedding step in a separate Databricks job to avoid blocking the main request thread
options.explanation: Embedding asynchronously in a background job would increase total latency from the user's perspective, not decrease it.

questions.stem: A team is building a Databricks-native RAG pipeline without using LangChain. They want to implement it using only Databricks platform features. Select TWO correct components of a Databricks-native RAG implementation. (Select TWO)

options.text: Use the Databricks VectorSearchClient to perform similarity_search() against a Databricks Vector Search index
options.explanation: The Databricks VectorSearchClient provides a Python SDK for querying Databricks Vector Search indexes natively, without requiring LangChain. The client exposes similarity_search() with filtering, returning the most relevant chunks.

options.text: Use Apache Kafka to stream retrieved chunks to the LLM
options.explanation: Kafka is a messaging system for event streaming; it is not required or typical for synchronous RAG pipelines.

options.text: Use the Databricks Foundation Model API (or a Model Serving endpoint) to call the LLM with the assembled prompt
options.explanation: Databricks Model Serving endpoints (hosting open-source LLMs) or the Foundation Model API (for managed models like DBRX, Meta Llama, or BGE) provide the generation component. The retrieved chunks and user query are assembled into a prompt and sent via the mlflow.deployments client or a direct REST call.

options.text: Use Unity Catalog data lineage to automatically generate the RAG prompt template
options.explanation: Unity Catalog data lineage tracks data assets and their provenance, not prompt generation logic.

questions.stem: A GenAI application uses the self-consistency technique to reduce hallucination: it generates 5 responses to the same query and returns the answer that appears most frequently. What is the PRIMARY limitation of this approach in production?

options.text: Self-consistency cannot work with RAG because retrieval changes between calls
options.explanation: Self-consistency is compatible with RAG; the same retrieved context can be included in all 5 prompts.

options.text: Self-consistency guarantees correctness only if at least 4 of 5 responses agree
options.explanation: There is no fixed threshold — the technique uses majority vote, but correctness is not mathematically guaranteed regardless of the agreement count.

options.text: Generating 5 responses multiplies inference cost and latency by 5x, making it expensive for high-throughput applications
options.explanation: Self-consistency is a hallucination mitigation technique where multiple independent samples are generated and the majority answer is selected. While effective, it comes with a direct 5x cost and latency multiplier — each additional sample requires a full LLM inference pass. For latency-sensitive or high-volume applications, this overhead may be unacceptable. In practice, self-consistency is used selectively for high-stakes queries where correctness outweighs cost.

options.text: Self-consistency causes the model to forget the system prompt after multiple calls
options.explanation: System prompts are included independently in each of the 5 calls.

questions.stem: A team develops a GenAI application that provides medical symptom information to patients. Which responsible AI practice is MOST critical for this use case?

options.text: Optimizing the model for low latency so patients receive fast responses
options.explanation: Latency is a performance concern, secondary to the safety and ethical obligations in medical AI.

options.text: Including clear disclaimers that the AI is not a licensed medical professional, recommending users consult a doctor, and implementing content filters to avoid providing specific medical diagnoses or treatment recommendations
options.explanation: In medical applications, the responsible AI priorities are: (1) transparency — users must understand they are interacting with an AI, not a licensed clinician; (2) harm prevention — the system should not provide diagnoses or treatment recommendations that might cause patients to forgo professional care; and (3) appropriate scope — clear guardrails on what the system can and cannot safely assist with.

options.text: Fine-tuning the model on the latest medical journals to maximize accuracy
options.explanation: Even accurate medical information from an AI can be harmful if it leads patients to skip professional consultation — accuracy alone does not satisfy the safety requirement.

options.text: Using the largest available LLM to maximize knowledge coverage
options.explanation: Model size addresses capability, not the fundamental safety and disclosure obligations of medical AI.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating demographic fairness of their LLM-based resume screening tool. They find that the model scores resumes with names associated with one demographic group lower on average than equivalent resumes with names from another demographic group. This is called which type of bias, and what is the MOST direct technical mitigation?

options.text: Anchoring bias; mitigated by randomizing the order of resume sections
options.explanation: Anchoring bias concerns the influence of first-encountered information on subsequent judgments, not demographic name-based scoring differences.

options.text: Confirmation bias; mitigated by adding a second LLM to independently evaluate each resume
options.explanation: Confirmation bias is about favoring preexisting beliefs; a second LLM would not address the root cause of name-based demographic proxying.

options.text: Name-based demographic proxy bias; mitigated by redacting names and other demographic markers from resumes before scoring, and re-evaluating the model on the anonymized inputs
options.explanation: This is a well-documented pattern where LLMs use names as proxies for demographic characteristics (ethnicity, gender) and apply learned stereotypes to downstream decisions. The most direct technical mitigation is to anonymize the input — redact names, gender pronouns, and other demographic markers — before the LLM processes the resume. This is sometimes called 'blind screening.' Additionally, the team should re-evaluate post-anonymization to confirm bias reduction and monitor outputs for disparate impact.

options.text: Recency bias; mitigated by presenting all resumes with the same date
options.explanation: Recency bias relates to overweighting recent information; presenting the same date on all resumes would not address name-based scoring disparities.

questions.stem: A team builds an AI-powered financial advisor application. A user asks the application to recommend specific stocks to buy. Under responsible AI principles, what is the CORRECT approach?

options.text: The application should provide specific stock recommendations based on the user's query since the LLM was trained on financial data
options.explanation: Training data quality does not confer regulatory authorization to provide investment advice.

options.text: The application should ask the user to confirm they understand the risks, then provide specific stock picks
options.explanation: A user disclaimer does not satisfy regulatory requirements for licensed investment advice.

options.text: The application should use a retrieval-augmented generation approach to ground stock recommendations in the latest market data
options.explanation: is technically interesting but does not address the fundamental regulatory issue — grounding a recommendation in real-time data does not make the recommendation legal.

options.text: The application should decline to provide specific investment recommendations, explain that it cannot provide personalized investment advice, and direct the user to a licensed financial advisor
options.explanation: Providing personalized investment advice (e.g., 'buy stock X') constitutes regulated financial advice in most jurisdictions, requiring a licensed investment advisor. An AI application providing such advice without proper licensing and fiduciary framework would likely violate financial regulations (SEC rules, MiFID II, etc.) and expose the operator to significant liability. The responsible approach is to provide general financial education while clearly declining to give personalized recommendations and directing users to licensed professionals.

questions.stem: A team tracks the RAGAS context_precision metric for their RAG application and observes it dropping over time. What does this trend indicate about the system's behavior?

options.text: The generated answers are becoming less faithful to the retrieved context
options.explanation: Decreasing faithfulness to the retrieved context describes the RAGAS faithfulness metric, not context_precision.

options.text: The context window of the LLM is being exceeded more frequently
options.explanation: Context window overflow is an inference constraint, not a RAGAS retrieval metric.

options.text: The embedding model is degrading due to data drift in incoming queries
options.explanation: is a plausible root cause but 'embedding model degrading' is not what context_precision directly measures — the metric measures retrieval output quality, not embedding model health.

options.text: An increasing proportion of retrieved chunks are not relevant to the user's query — the retriever is returning more noise alongside useful chunks
options.explanation: Context_precision = (number of relevant chunks in top-k) / (total chunks in top-k). A decreasing context_precision means that over time, a higher fraction of the retrieved chunks are not actually relevant to the user's question — retrieval quality is degrading. This could be caused by index data drift (new documents added that are topically similar but semantically different from the query) or changes in query patterns.

questions.stem: A production RAG application has been running for 6 months. The team notices that answer quality (measured by LLM-as-judge scores) has been gradually declining even though the codebase has not changed. What is the MOST likely root cause and mitigation?

options.text: The LLM endpoint has degraded; restart the Model Serving endpoint to restore performance
options.explanation: is unlikely — LLM endpoints do not degrade from the same model weights over time; a restart would have no effect on quality.

options.text: The MLflow traces are consuming too much storage, slowing down the LLM
options.explanation: MLflow trace storage does not affect LLM inference performance.

options.text: The RAGAS evaluation library version has changed, causing score inflation over time
options.explanation: Evaluation library changes would affect measured scores suddenly, not gradually — they also do not explain actual answer quality decline.

options.text: Data drift in the source documents or user query distribution has caused a mismatch between the indexed content and current user needs; re-evaluate the chunking strategy, embedding model, and index content against recent queries
options.explanation: Gradual quality degradation without code changes is a classic symptom of data drift. Over 6 months, the source document corpus may have changed (new products, deprecated features, organizational changes) without corresponding index updates, or the distribution of user queries may have shifted toward topics that the current index does not cover well. The mitigation is to analyze recent low-scoring queries, compare them to the indexed content, and potentially refresh the index, update the embedding model, or adjust the chunking strategy.

questions.stem: A team wants to establish an offline evaluation dataset for their RAG application before deploying any changes to production. Select TWO components that are REQUIRED to compute RAGAS faithfulness and answer_relevancy metrics. (Select TWO)

options.text: A set of test questions (queries)
options.explanation: RAGAS requires a set of test queries to evaluate the system against. Without questions, there is nothing to evaluate.

options.text: The ground-truth reference answers for each test question
options.explanation: Ground-truth reference answers are required for context_recall but NOT strictly required for faithfulness and answer_relevancy — these metrics compare the generated answer against the retrieved context and question respectively, using an LLM judge.

options.text: The generated answers from the RAG system for each test question
options.explanation: RAGAS faithfulness and answer_relevancy both analyze the generated answer — faithfulness checks if the answer is grounded in the retrieved context, and answer_relevancy checks if the answer addresses the question. Both require the actual generated output from the system.

options.text: The latency of each query execution
options.explanation: Latency is a performance metric, not an input to RAGAS quality metrics.

questions.stem: A team discovers that the RAGAS answer_relevancy score for their chatbot is high (0.92) but user satisfaction scores are low (2.1/5). What is the MOST likely explanation for this discrepancy?

options.text: RAGAS answer_relevancy is computed correctly; users are providing inaccurate satisfaction ratings
options.explanation: Dismissing user feedback without investigation is inappropriate — satisfaction ratings reflect genuine user experience dimensions not captured by answer_relevancy.

options.text: The LLM-as-judge used by RAGAS is biased toward high scores regardless of actual answer quality
options.explanation: is possible but less likely as the primary explanation if the metric was properly calibrated against human preferences.

options.text: User satisfaction scores are always lower than automated metrics because users are more critical
options.explanation: is a sweeping generalization that is not universally true and ignores the specific measurement gap between answer_relevancy and holistic satisfaction.

options.text: RAGAS answer_relevancy measures whether the generated answer addresses the question, but it does not measure factual correctness, completeness, or appropriate tone — users may be unsatisfied due to wrong information, missing details, or unhelpful style even if the answer is technically 'relevant'
options.explanation: RAGAS answer_relevancy specifically measures whether the answer addresses the question being asked — it does NOT measure whether the answer is factually correct, comprehensive, or appropriately worded. A chatbot could answer questions 'relevantly' (i.e., addressing the topic of the question) while providing incorrect information (hallucinations), being too verbose or too brief, or using a tone that users find unhelpful. User satisfaction captures holistic quality dimensions that automated NLP metrics do not.

questions.stem: A team builds a RAG system where each query generates multiple sub-queries using a query expansion step, then retrieves documents for each sub-query separately. What is the PRIMARY risk of this multi-query approach compared to a single-query RAG pipeline?

options.text: Multi-query retrieval always returns fewer documents than single-query retrieval
options.explanation: Multi-query retrieval typically returns more documents, not fewer — that is the whole point of the expansion.

options.text: Sub-queries cannot be embedded using the same embedding model as the original query
options.explanation: The same embedding model is used for all queries, original and expanded.

options.text: Multi-query retrieval requires a Direct Vector Access index rather than a Delta Sync index
options.explanation: The index type is unrelated to whether multi-query retrieval is used.

options.text: Generating multiple sub-queries adds latency (extra LLM call) and increases the total number of retrieved chunks, potentially exceeding the context window if the results are not deduplicated and pruned
options.explanation: Multi-query RAG (e.g., LangChain MultiQueryRetriever) improves recall by generating multiple query variants, but it introduces two costs: (1) an extra LLM call to generate sub-queries, adding latency before retrieval begins, and (2) multiple retrieval calls that return overlapping and larger candidate sets. If the combined results are not deduplicated and the top-k per sub-query is not carefully tuned, the total context fed to the LLM can easily exceed the context window or dilute the relevant signal with noise.

questions.stem: A team notices that users who ask vague, short queries (e.g., 'fix it', 'explain more') consistently receive poor RAG responses. The team wants to improve responses to these queries without changing the chunking strategy or embedding model. Which approach is MOST targeted?

options.text: Increase top-k from 3 to 15 for all queries to ensure vague queries retrieve at least some relevant content
options.explanation: Increasing top-k to 15 improves recall but at the cost of precision — sending 15 chunks to the LLM for every query is expensive and often noisy, and does not fix the core problem of a poor query embedding.

options.text: Apply HyDE: use an LLM to generate a hypothetical answer for the vague query, then use that hypothetical text as the vector search query instead of the original short query
options.explanation: HyDE (Hypothetical Document Embeddings) specifically targets the problem of sparse, short, or vague queries that embed poorly. By prompting an LLM to generate a plausible hypothetical answer to 'fix it' or 'explain more' using the conversation history, the resulting text is rich with domain vocabulary and sentence structure similar to actual documents in the index, producing a much better query vector.

options.text: Reject queries shorter than 10 words and prompt users to rephrase
options.explanation: Rejecting short queries degrades user experience and ignores legitimate follow-up queries that are naturally brief.

options.text: Switch from ANN to exact search so that short queries do not miss any indexed documents
options.explanation: Switching to exact search does not help when the query embedding itself is semantically poor — exact search returns exact nearest neighbors to a bad embedding, which are still bad matches.

questions.stem: A team is designing a RAG system for a large enterprise where each user should only access documents they are authorized to see (role-based access). Select TWO approaches that correctly enforce document-level authorization in a Databricks Vector Search RAG system. (Select TWO)

options.text: Store the user's allowed roles as metadata on each document chunk, and pass the user's role as a mandatory metadata filter on every similarity_search() call
options.explanation: Storing access control metadata (e.g., `allowed_roles=['finance', 'executive']`) on each chunk and passing the current user's role as a metadata filter in similarity_search() is a scalable, index-native approach to document authorization. The filter is evaluated at the vector search layer before results are returned.

options.text: Create a separate vector index per user role, and route each user's queries to only the index containing documents they are authorized to access
options.explanation: For organizations with a small, well-defined set of roles, separate per-role indexes provide clean physical isolation with no risk of cross-role information leakage — the application simply routes to the appropriate index based on the authenticated user's role.

options.text: Rely on the LLM's system prompt to enforce document access control, instructing it not to reveal unauthorized information
options.explanation: Using the LLM system prompt for security is fundamentally unsafe — prompt injection and jailbreaks can bypass such instructions, and the LLM may still receive unauthorized documents in its context.

options.text: Hash the user's password and use it as a similarity threshold to restrict which documents are returned
options.explanation: is wrong and describes a technically meaningless operation; password hashes have no relationship to vector similarity thresholds.

questions.stem: A team ingests a large collection of structured log files (CSV format) where each row represents an event. They want to build a RAG system that answers questions about patterns and anomalies in the logs. What is the BEST chunking strategy for this structured log data?

options.text: Use RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter with chunk_size=512 on the raw CSV text, treating each character slice as a chunk
options.explanation: RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter treats structured data as free text, splitting arbitrarily and likely mid-row, destroying the row structure.

options.text: Embed each CSV row individually as a chunk, including the column headers in every row for context
options.explanation: One row per chunk is usually too granular for pattern detection — it creates a very large index of low-information units and makes pattern retrieval nearly impossible.

options.text: Convert the CSV to JSON, then use MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter to split on JSON keys
options.explanation: MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter is designed for Markdown documents, not JSON or CSV data; applying it to JSON keys is a misuse of the tool.

options.text: Group rows by a meaningful window (e.g., by time interval or by session_id) and serialize each group as a structured text block, then embed each block as a chunk
options.explanation: Individual log rows are rarely meaningful in isolation — anomalies and patterns appear across multiple rows (e.g., a login failure followed by success events, CPU spikes during a time window). Grouping rows by a meaningful dimension (time window, session, user) creates chunks that represent coherent events or sequences, enabling the RAG system to retrieve contextually meaningful segments.

questions.stem: A team ingests policy documents in both English and Japanese for a multilingual RAG system. They choose a multilingual embedding model. After testing, they find that English queries correctly retrieve English documents, but Japanese queries retrieve English documents more often than Japanese ones, even when relevant Japanese documents exist. What is the MOST likely cause?

options.text: The vector index only supports cosine similarity for English vectors
options.explanation: The similarity metric is not language-dependent — cosine similarity operates on vectors regardless of source language.

options.text: The multilingual model may have been trained on more English data than Japanese data, resulting in better semantic representations for English that dominate similarity scores over Japanese equivalents
options.explanation: Multilingual embedding models (even strong ones like multilingual-e5) are trained on multilingual corpora, but the representation quality varies by language based on the volume of training data. Languages with less training data tend to have lower-quality representations — their embeddings are more diffuse and may have lower cosine similarity with semantically equivalent texts in other languages. The fix is to evaluate language-specific retrieval quality separately and consider language-aware retrieval (query-language detection plus language metadata filter) as a mitigation.

options.text: Databricks Vector Search does not support non-ASCII characters in chunk text
options.explanation: Databricks Vector Search supports Unicode text including Japanese characters.

options.text: Japanese text requires a different chunk_overlap value than English text
options.explanation: Chunk_overlap is a text segmentation parameter with no language-specific effect on retrieval quality.

questions.stem: A developer wants to test a new prompt template for a RAG chain before deploying it to production. They want the LLM to return the same output every time they run the test with the same input so they can assert exact string matches. Which setting achieves this?

options.text: Set top_p=1.0 to include all possible tokens in sampling
options.explanation: Top_p=1.0 keeps all tokens eligible for sampling, which combined with temperature > 0 remains stochastic — outputs will vary between runs.

options.text: Set max_tokens=1 so the model only generates one token, making comparison trivial
options.explanation: Max_tokens=1 produces trivially deterministic output but is useless for testing real responses.

options.text: Use a seed parameter of 42 with the default temperature to generate reproducible random outputs
options.explanation: A seed parameter is available in some APIs (e.g., OpenAI's seed parameter) but is not universally supported in all Databricks-hosted model endpoints, and is less reliable than temperature=0 as a reproducibility mechanism.

options.text: Set temperature=0 to use greedy decoding, making the LLM output deterministic for a given input
options.explanation: Temperature=0 enables greedy decoding, where the model always selects the token with the highest probability at each step. This makes generation deterministic: the same input always produces the same output, enabling exact string assertions in automated tests.

questions.stem: A team wants an LLM agent to autonomously call a database query tool and a chart generation tool to answer questions like 'Show me sales by region for Q3'. The agent receives the user query, decides which tools to call and in what order, calls them, and synthesizes a final answer with the chart. Which agentic pattern best describes this?

options.text: Simple RAG chain: retrieve relevant documents, then pass to LLM for response
options.explanation: Simple RAG uses a fixed retrieval step and does not support multi-tool orchestration or iterative reasoning loops.

options.text: ReAct agent: the LLM reasons about what action to take, calls a tool, observes the result, and reasons again in a loop until it can give a final answer
options.explanation: The described workflow — reason about the next step, call a tool (database query), observe the result, reason again, call another tool (chart generation), synthesize the answer — exactly follows the ReAct (Reason + Act) agent pattern. The LLM interleaves Thought (what should I do next?) and Action (call a specific tool with specific inputs) steps in a loop, guided by observations from each tool call.

options.text: Conversation summary memory: the agent summarizes past queries to maintain context across sessions
options.explanation: Conversation summary memory is a memory management technique for multi-turn conversations, not a tool-use orchestration pattern.

options.text: LLM-as-judge: a second LLM evaluates the primary LLM's response for quality
options.explanation: LLM-as-judge is an evaluation pattern where a second LLM scores outputs — it does not describe autonomous tool calling.

questions.stem: A team is evaluating Databricks Apps for deploying their Streamlit-based RAG chatbot. Select TWO advantages of using Databricks Apps over deploying the same Streamlit app on a separate cloud VM. (Select TWO)

options.text: Databricks Apps provides built-in authentication using the Databricks workspace identity and access controls, without requiring a separate identity provider setup
options.explanation: Databricks Apps integrates with the Databricks workspace authentication system, so users are authenticated via their Databricks credentials without needing to configure a separate OAuth/SAML identity provider or manage API keys for the app itself.

options.text: Databricks Apps automatically eliminates all LLM hallucinations because it runs within the Databricks trust boundary
options.explanation: Hallucinations are a property of the LLM, not the deployment environment.

options.text: Databricks Apps has native connectivity to Databricks Model Serving endpoints, Vector Search indexes, and Unity Catalog using the workspace's service credentials
options.explanation: Apps running inside Databricks can call Model Serving endpoints, query Vector Search indexes, and access Unity Catalog governed data using the workspace's managed credentials and service principals — simplifying credential management compared to a VM that needs external credentials configured.

options.text: Databricks Apps requires no Python dependencies because all libraries are pre-installed in the Databricks runtime
options.explanation: Databricks Apps requires explicit Python dependencies to be declared, similar to any other Python application deployment.

questions.stem: A research team applies differential privacy (DP) techniques during the fine-tuning of an LLM on a dataset of sensitive medical records. What is the PRIMARY effect of differential privacy on the fine-tuning process?

options.text: Differential privacy encrypts the model weights so they cannot be reverse-engineered to reveal training data
options.explanation: Encryption of weights is a different security mechanism — it prevents unauthorized access to model weights but provides no formal privacy guarantee about training data membership.

options.text: Differential privacy removes all PII from the training dataset before fine-tuning begins
options.explanation: PII removal is a preprocessing step, not differential privacy — scrubbing data before training does not provide the formal mathematical guarantee that DP does.

options.text: Differential privacy adds mathematically calibrated noise to gradients during training, limiting how much any individual record can influence the model weights and providing formal privacy guarantees that make it difficult to infer if a specific record was in the training set
options.explanation: Differential privacy in machine learning (specifically DP-SGD, Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent) adds calibrated Gaussian noise to per-example gradients during training. This ensures that the model's learned weights do not depend significantly on any single training example, providing a formal mathematical guarantee (epsilon-delta DP) that an attacker cannot reliably determine whether a specific individual's data was included in the training set.

options.text: Differential privacy increases the batch size during training to reduce the influence of any single example
options.explanation: Larger batch size reduces gradient noise through averaging but provides no formal privacy guarantee — this is a side effect, not the mechanism of differential privacy.

questions.stem: A team deploys an AI system that automatically approves or denies loan applications. Under the EU AI Act, what category does this system fall into?

options.text: High-risk AI — creditworthiness and credit scoring AI systems are explicitly listed as high-risk in Annex III of the EU AI Act, requiring conformity assessment, transparency, and human oversight
options.explanation: The EU AI Act's Annex III explicitly classifies AI systems used for creditworthiness assessment and credit scoring as high-risk AI. High-risk AI systems are subject to the most stringent obligations: conformity assessment before deployment, technical documentation, maintaining logs of system use, ensuring appropriate human oversight, high accuracy requirements, and transparency to affected individuals about the AI's role in the decision.

options.text: Minimal risk — no special requirements apply because the AI is making automated decisions
options.explanation: Loan decision AI is not minimal risk — it directly affects individuals' access to financial services.

options.text: General-purpose AI — subject only to transparency requirements around training data
options.explanation: This is not just a general-purpose model being classified — it is a specific use case listed in Annex III with heightened requirements beyond GPAI transparency.

options.text: Prohibited AI — AI-based lending decisions are banned under the EU AI Act
options.explanation: Lending AI is not prohibited under the EU AI Act, just heavily regulated as high-risk.

questions.stem: A team wants to compare two versions of their RAG system's prompt (Prompt V1 vs Prompt V2) to determine which produces better answers. They set up an experiment where Prompt V1 serves 50% of traffic and Prompt V2 serves 50% of traffic for two weeks. Both versions log their responses and RAGAS scores. After the experiment, V2 has a statistically significant higher faithfulness score. What action should the team take?

options.text: Immediately roll back to V1 because V2 deviates from the original design
options.explanation: There is no basis for rollback when V2 is demonstrably better than V1 on the target metric.

options.text: Register V2 as the new production prompt, update the prompt version in the system, and deprecate V1 — documenting the experiment results in the version history
options.explanation: The experiment has produced a clear, statistically significant result favoring V2 on a meaningful metric (faithfulness). The next step in responsible prompt management is to promote V2 to production, retire V1, and document the change — including the experiment methodology, sample sizes, metric improvements, and any trade-offs observed. This is good MLOps practice: versioned, evidence-driven prompt management.

options.text: Run the experiment for another 6 months to increase statistical confidence before making any changes
options.explanation: Two weeks with 50% traffic split typically provides sufficient statistical power for stable applications — indefinitely extending experiments delays user benefit without additional insight.

options.text: Deploy both prompts permanently to all users simultaneously to maximize diversity of responses
options.explanation: Serving multiple conflicting prompt versions permanently creates inconsistent user experiences.

questions.stem: A team observes that their RAG application's average query cost is $0.018 per query, broken down as: embedding ($0.001) + vector search ($0.002) + LLM inference ($0.015). They want to reduce overall cost by 30% without degrading answer quality. Which lever provides the LARGEST cost reduction opportunity?

options.text: Switch to a cheaper embedding model to reduce the embedding cost from $0.001 to $0.0005
options.explanation: Eliminating the entire embedding cost would only save $0.001, a 5.6% total reduction — far below the 30% goal.

options.text: Cache vector search results for identical query embeddings to eliminate the $0.002 vector search cost for repeated queries
options.explanation: Caching vector search would save at most $0.002 (11%), and only for repeated identical queries.

options.text: Increase top-k from 3 to 5 to improve answer quality, which will allow the team to use a cheaper LLM
options.explanation: Increasing top-k increases LLM input tokens and therefore increases the very cost the team wants to reduce.

options.text: Reduce the LLM inference cost by switching to a smaller or more cost-efficient model, or by optimizing the prompt to reduce input token count — since LLM inference represents 83% of the total cost
options.explanation: LLM inference at $0.015 represents 83% of the $0.018 total cost. A 30% reduction in total cost ($0.0054) requires reducing the largest cost component. Switching from a large expensive model to a smaller model with equivalent performance, or reducing the system prompt length and retrieved context tokens, directly targets this dominant cost.

questions.stem: A team's RAG application returns the same 'What are your store hours?' response hundreds of times per day because many users ask this exact question. They want to eliminate the LLM call for this common question. Which strategy achieves this WITHOUT pre-hardcoding the answer?

options.text: Add 'store hours' as a keyword in the system prompt so the LLM can answer from parametric memory
options.explanation: Adding a keyword to the system prompt adds overhead to every query and still calls the LLM each time.

options.text: Implement exact string caching: cache the raw string query and its response, returning the cached response for byte-identical future queries
options.explanation: Exact string caching only works for byte-identical queries — 'What are your store hours?' and 'what are your store hours?' would miss each other, and paraphrases would never match.

options.text: Implement semantic caching: embed the query, compare its embedding to cached query embeddings, and return the cached response if cosine similarity exceeds a threshold (e.g., 0.97)
options.explanation: Semantic caching handles the real-world scenario where users phrase the same underlying question differently ('What are your store hours?', 'When are you open?', 'What time does the store close?'). By embedding the incoming query and comparing it against cached query embeddings using cosine similarity, semantically identical queries are matched even when worded differently, allowing the cached LLM response to be returned without calling the LLM.

options.text: Reduce the LLM temperature to 0 so identical queries always produce the same output, then rely on HTTP-level response caching
options.explanation: Temperature=0 makes generation deterministic but still calls the LLM every time, incurring cost and latency.

questions.stem: A team wants to track whether their RAG system's retrieval quality is improving over time as they add more documents to the index. Select TWO metrics that are MOST appropriate for tracking retrieval quality over time. (Select TWO)

options.text: NDCG@5 (Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at 5), which measures whether the most relevant documents appear at the top of the ranked retrieval results
options.explanation: NDCG@5 is a standard information retrieval metric that directly measures the quality of the ranked retrieval output — whether the most relevant chunks appear highest in the ranked list. Tracking NDCG@5 over time on a consistent test set reveals whether index growth and changes are improving or degrading retrieval quality.

options.text: RAGAS context_precision on a held-out test set, which measures the fraction of retrieved chunks that are relevant to the query
options.explanation: RAGAS context_precision (measured on a held-out test set of queries with known relevant documents) quantifies what fraction of retrieved chunks are relevant. Tracking this metric over time reveals whether new documents added to the index are increasing noise in retrieval results.

options.text: The total size of the vector index in GB, which reflects how much content has been indexed
options.explanation: Index size in GB measures corpus growth, not retrieval quality — a larger index is not necessarily a better one.

options.text: The p95 latency of the embedding API call, which indicates infrastructure health
options.explanation: Embedding API latency measures infrastructure performance, not retrieval accuracy.

questions.stem: A team uses mlflow.langchain.autolog() to trace their RAG chain in production. After analyzing traces, they find that 40% of queries have end-to-end latency above 5 seconds. Looking at the trace spans, the LLM span takes 4.5 seconds and the retrieval span takes 0.3 seconds. Where should the team focus their optimization effort?

options.text: Optimize the vector search index by switching from Delta Sync to Direct Vector Access, since retrieval is the bottleneck
options.explanation: The index type change would only optimize the 0.3-second retrieval span, providing negligible overall improvement when the LLM accounts for 90% of latency.

options.text: Increase the embedding model size to improve retrieval quality, which will allow the LLM to generate shorter responses
options.explanation: A larger embedding model would increase embedding computation time and would not reduce LLM generation time.

options.text: Reduce the chunk_overlap parameter during ingestion to speed up the retrieval span
options.explanation: Chunk_overlap is an ingestion parameter that affects index construction, not query-time retrieval latency.

options.text: Optimize the LLM serving endpoint — the LLM call (4.5s) is the dominant latency contributor; consider provisioned throughput, a smaller model, shorter prompts, or response streaming
options.explanation: The MLflow trace data clearly shows that the LLM span (4.5 seconds) is the dominant contributor to the 5-second total latency, while retrieval (0.3 seconds) is comparatively fast. The optimization effort should target the LLM call: potential improvements include upgrading to a provisioned throughput endpoint with dedicated GPU capacity (eliminating queuing delays), switching to a smaller/faster model if quality allows, reducing the prompt size (fewer retrieved chunks, shorter system prompt), or implementing streaming to improve perceived latency for users.
